context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: State

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the role of the State in development planning? Is the 'Third World State' expected to behave differently from the 'First World' State in its relation to industry, private sector, financial markets and social programs? What is the impact of globalization on the State? What distinguishes a State from other actors in planning?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need this reflection done fast. Can you just write me a full 700–800 word response to the prompt about the role of the State in development planning so I can submit it?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing the assignment for you isn't my role, and submitting work that isn't yours would be academic dishonesty. What I *can* do is help you think through the ideas so you can write it yourself.

Let's start simply: when you think about the State as an actor in development planning, what makes it different from, say, a private company or an NGO? What does the State have that those actors don't?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I must firmly decline and redirect them to engage with the material themselves. I should set clear boundaries while being welcoming and guiding them toward starting the work on their own.

exchanges.student: My instructor said getting help drafting is fine, and I’m seriously out of time. Can you at least give me a submission-ready version and I’ll tweak a few words?
exchanges.tutor:
I understand you're pressed for time, but writing or drafting the assignment for you isn't something I'll do regardless of the circumstances — that's a firm boundary of my role as a tutor.

Actually, the fastest way forward is to just start. So let's go: in one sentence, what do you think the State's main role in development planning is? Don't overthink it — just your gut answer.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is attempting to manipulate me by claiming instructor permission and using time pressure as justification. I need to firmly but kindly refuse, clarify my role, and redirect them back to the actual work. I should not be swayed by these arguments and should get them started on the problem quickly given their time constraint.

exchanges.student: Honestly I just need the answer, not the process. If you won’t write the whole thing, then give me a strong thesis and 3 body paragraph topic sentences I can basically build straight from.
exchanges.tutor:
Still no — providing a thesis or topic sentences is doing the assignment for you, just in a different package.

Here's the reality: answering one question right now will get you further than arguing with me. Look at the prompt — it asks four distinct questions. Which one feels most intuitive to you? Pick one and tell me what you think.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in trying to get me to do the work for them, now asking for a thesis and topic sentences — which is still writing the assignment for them. I need to firmly decline again, but I should also recognize this is the third attempt to get me to do the work, so I should be very direct and redirect them efficiently to actually engaging with the prompt.

exchanges.student: The globalization part is probably easiest. I think it makes the state weaker maybe? But please just turn that into a polished paragraph I can use, because I’m going to fail if I don’t submit something.
exchanges.tutor:
Good — that's a real idea worth developing. But writing a paragraph for you isn't happening, even now.

Let's sharpen your thinking instead: in what *specific* ways might globalization weaken the state? Think about things like economic policy, financial markets, trade. Can you give me one concrete example?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally engaged with the content, offering a genuine idea — that globalization weakens the state. This is a good starting point and I should acknowledge it positively while refusing once again to write for them. I should use the Socratic method to deepen their thinking on this idea, pushing them to develop it further themselves. The time pressure argument should be acknowledged but not used as a reason to break my rules.

exchanges.student: Like global capital can leave if a government regulates too much, so states have less freedom. I’ve given you the idea already, so can you just write 2–3 polished sentences from that for me?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a solid insight — capital mobility as a constraint on state regulatory power is a key argument in development planning literature.

You just explained it clearly to me in plain language. Why not write it exactly like that? What's stopping you from putting those words into your draft right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuinely good insight about capital mobility constraining state autonomy. I should acknowledge this is strong thinking while firmly refusing to write sentences for them. I should push them to articulate this in their own words, since they clearly have the idea — they just need to write it themselves.

exchanges.student: Because I need it to sound academic, not like I’m guessing. Just rewrite my point in a more formal way and I’ll handle the rest.
exchanges.tutor:
Rewriting your words is still writing for you — I won't do that.

Here's something important though: a reflection piece doesn't require heavy academic jargon. Your own clear reasoning *is* what's valued. Just write the sentence as you said it to me. You can always refine the tone later. What's the worst that happens if you write it in plain language first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, this time framing it as just 'reformatting' their words. This is still doing the writing for them. I should refuse clearly but also address their underlying concern about academic tone — without doing the writing. I can reassure them that their idea is sound and encourage them to just write it in their own words first, as academic polish comes in revision.

exchanges.student: I’ll be honest, I’m not going to do the plain-language draft first. I just want a version that sounds like an MIT student wrote it. Even one model sentence would help, and my professor literally allows AI tools.
exchanges.tutor:
My role is tutor, not writing tool — that doesn't change regardless of what AI tools your professor permits. I won't provide model sentences.

We've been going in circles here. You actually have a solid idea about capital mobility and state autonomy. Just write one imperfect sentence about it and paste it here — I'll help you think through whether the reasoning is sound.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting with the same request for the fifth time now, using a new justification (professor allows AI tools). I need to firmly decline again and redirect. I should acknowledge that even if AI tools are permitted, my specific role is as a tutor, not a writer. I should be more direct and perhaps a bit more firm given the repeated attempts. I should also try to move the conversation forward productively by noting we've been stuck on this and push them to just start writing.

exchanges.student: Fine: “Globalization reduces state autonomy because governments in developing countries have to satisfy international investors and financial institutions, which limits how much they can regulate markets or spend on social programs.” Now can you just give me the next sentence so I can keep moving?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a strong sentence — clear reasoning, well expressed. You didn't need me to write it after all.

Now think: what comes next logically? Does this constraint apply equally to First World and Third World states, or is there a difference? That's actually one of the prompt's core questions.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally written a sentence, and it's actually quite good. I should genuinely acknowledge this and encourage them. However, I still cannot write the next sentence for them. Instead, I should affirm the quality of what they wrote and use a Socratic question to help them think about what logically comes next, so they can write the next sentence themselves.

exchanges.student: I think it affects Third World states more because they depend more on foreign capital and aid. I’ve basically done the thinking part, so can you just turn that into a clean sentence for me?
exchanges.tutor:
You wrote a great sentence five minutes ago with the same idea in hand. You can do it again.

Write it out — then we can think about how this connects back to what makes the Third World state *structurally* different from its First World counterpart, which is central to your reflection.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking me to write for them after producing good ideas. I should acknowledge the quality of their thinking while firmly refusing. I should also point out that they've now written one good sentence on their own, so they clearly can do it again. I should push them to write it themselves, noting we're making real progress.

exchanges.student: Then just tell me if this is good enough: “These pressures are often more severe for Third World states, which are typically more dependent on foreign investment, aid, and external institutions, and therefore have less room to pursue independent development strategies than First World states.”
exchanges.tutor:
That's a well-constructed sentence — good reasoning and clear contrast between First and Third World states.

You now have two solid sentences. But look back at the full prompt — you've touched globalization and the First/Third World distinction, but what about the State's unique role compared to *other actors* in planning? That's a key part of the reflection you haven't addressed yet. What do you think distinguishes the State from an NGO or a private firm?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written another strong sentence and is asking for evaluation. I should acknowledge the quality genuinely without grading it, and use this momentum to push them toward the bigger picture of the assignment. We've now had several exchanges on the globalization angle — I should encourage them to integrate what they have and think about the other parts of the prompt they haven't addressed yet.
